title: Symmetry as a route to generalized bosonic Kitaev chains
abstract: The bosonic Kitaev chain (BKC) model is a deceptively simple looking quadratic pairing Hamiltonian. Despite being purely Hermitian, it exhibits a number of striking non-Hermitian topological phenomena, including skin effects. We show here how symmetries play a key role in this model, and how identifying these allows one to develop generalized BKC-like models. We emphasize the surprising fact that any quadratic bosonic pairing Hamiltonian with a sublattice (chiral) symmetry necessarily has a dynamical matrix with an effective time reversal symmetry. This symmetry is unrelated to physical time-reversal, but enables non-trivial topological invariants. We also discuss how this symmetry is unrelated to another key property of the BKC, the decoupling of quadrature dynamics. This feature can instead be connected to a distinct symmetry, namely an effective particle-hole symmetry of the dynamical matrix. We discuss non-trivial generalized BKC models that only keep one of these two effective symmetries intact. We also provide a classification of all translationally-invariant 1D pairing Hamiltonians, and show connections between the BKC and a well-studied non-Hermitian fermionic system, the symplectic Hatano-Nelson model.

bite.hook: A hidden effective time-reversal symmetry is mathematically guaranteed in any chiral quadratic bosonic pairing system.
tldr: The authors analyzed the role of symmetries in the bosonic Kitaev chain (BKC) to construct generalized topological models.
They mathematically classified 1D translationally-invariant pairing Hamiltonians and mapped the BKC to the Hatano-Nelson model.
They discovered that sublattice symmetry forces an effective time-reversal symmetry, enabling non-trivial topological invariants.
bite.whyItMatters: Understanding these hidden symmetries allows physicists to design new topological quantum materials and devices, like robust one-way amplifiers, using bosons (like light or sound) instead of fermions.
bite.eli5: Normally, waves like light travel equally in both directions. By finding hidden mathematical symmetries in a chain of quantum particles, scientists can design 'one-way streets' where waves can only travel forward, even if you try to bounce them back.

glossary.shortDef: A 1D model of coupled bosons exhibiting non-Hermitian topological phases and skin effects.
glossary.eli5Def: A chain of quantum particles (bosons) that can host strange phenomena, like all of its waves piling up at one end.

glossary.shortDef: Describing quantum systems that gain or lose energy, leading to complex energy eigenvalues.
glossary.eli5Def: A way to describe quantum systems that leak energy to, or absorb energy from, the outside world.

glossary.shortDef: The localization of an extensive number of eigenstates at the boundaries of a non-Hermitian system.
glossary.eli5Def: A strange quantum crowding effect where almost all the waves in a material get squeezed onto its outer edge.

glossary.shortDef: A symmetry of a Hamiltonian that anti-commutes with a unitary operator, often splitting a lattice into two sublattices.
glossary.eli5Def: A structural balance in a grid of atoms where you can split them into two alternating groups that mirror each other's behavior.
